phenomenon: failed neural compensation to chronic challenge
claim: Addiction arises not from pathological gain-of-function adaptation but from inability to restore normal plasticity after drug-induced impairment.
Incidental touch properties cue associated concepts and apply them predictably to unrelated social and decisional targets through metaphorically grounded conceptual links.

Neural responses to syntactic violations in language and harmonic violations in music are indistinguishable, suggesting a shared mechanism for processing rule-based sequential structure independent of domain.

Managers penalize high-capability signals in hiring because they infer lower organizational commitment and higher employee mobility, creating a ceiling effect where capability above sufficiency becomes disadvantageous.

phenomenon: coexistence of scientific and religious worldviews
claim: Roughly one-fifth of the U.S. public integrates favorable views of both science and religion by narrowing conflict to specific origin-of-life questions while accepting scientific knowledge on other topics, suggesting epistemological rather than ideological boundaries.

phenomenon: perceptual vs. conceptual price processing modes
claim: Left-digit pricing biases emerge from stimulus-based perceptual digit-by-digit comparison and dissipate under memory-based evaluation relying on conceptual magnitude rounding, with stronger effects when reference prices are salient or category knowledge is limited.
